TheScrasse's blog

Editorial of Pinely Round 3 (Div. 1 + Div. 2)
By TheScrasse, history, 2 years ago,

The official implementations of all the problems are here.

Timeline of the round proposal (may contain spoilers)

(problems D', H', etc. were not used)

  • Feb 19, 2022: I proposed problem D' to Codeforces Round 778 (Div. 1 + Div. 2, based on Technocup 2022 Final Round), but it was not used.
  • Mar 13, 2023: I invented problem A.
  • May 16: I invented problem H'.
  • May 19: I realized problem G can be solved in O(n)O(n) time and we used it in the Italian team selection test for IOI.
  • Jul 04: I opened a Div. 1 proposal containing A, D', G, H' and other problems which are not going to be used.
  • Aug 09: I invented problem I, with intended solution in O(n3)O(n3).
  • Sep 27: I invented problem C.
  • Oct 14: errorgorn replied to my contest proposal.
  • Nov 02: I invented problem D''. errorgorn solved problem I in O(n2logn)O(n2log⁡n).
  • Nov 08: I invented problems E and F. I didn't propose problem F because I thought it was too easy.
  • Nov 10: I invented problem H with O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) operations. We had a preliminary problemset, with problems A, D', C, D'', E, H1, G, H', I. I started preparing the problems.
  • Nov 14: I finished preparing the problems, and the round was ready for testing. dario2994 tested and didn't solve problem D''. I realized the official solution of problem D'' was wrong (and now I don't have any solution).
  • Nov 15: I removed problem D'' from the contest and replaced it with problem F.
  • Nov 17: I invented problem E'.
  • Nov 19: I invented problem F', which was rejected because it was proposed independently to errorgorn by the authors of another round.
  • Nov 22: gamegame tested and said problem H' is well-known in China. I removed it.
  • Nov 26: we realized problem D' was too hard for position B.
  • Nov 28: I invented problem D with k=1k=1 and ai≥2ai≥2, and I proposed it as B.
  • Nov 29: I invented problem B. Endagorion solved problem H in 2n2n operations.
  • Dec 07: the problemset was A, B, C, D', F1, F2, E, G, H1, H2, I, but there was a huge gap between D' and the next problems. We ended up using A, B, C, D, E', E, F2, G, H2, I (i.e., 10 problems). D still had k=1k=1 and ai≥2ai≥2.
  • Dec 13: we removed the constraints k=1k=1 and ai≥2ai≥2 from problem D.
  • Dec 18: problem E' appeared in Educational Codeforces Round 160 (Rated for Div. 2). We removed it and we decided to use subtasks on problem F.

1909A - Distinct Buttons

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Suppose you can only use UU, RR, DD. Which cells can you reach?

Solution

If you only use buttons UU, RR, DD, you can never reach the points with x<0x<0. However, if all the special points have x≥0x≥0, you can reach all of them with the following steps:

  • visit all the special points with x=0x=0, using the buttons UU, DD;
  • press the button RR to reach x=1x=1;
  • visit all the special points with x=1x=1, using the buttons UU, DD;
  • ……
  • visit all the special points with x=100x=100, using the buttons UU, DD.

Similarly, if you use at most 33 buttons in total, you can reach all the special points if at least one of the following conditions is true:

  • all xi≥0xi≥0;
  • all xi≤0xi≤0;
  • all yi≥0yi≥0;
  • all yi≤0yi≤0.

Complexity: O(n)O(n)

1909B - Make Almost Equal With Mod

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Find a value of kk that works in many cases.

Hint 2

k=2k=2 works in many cases. What if it does not work?

Hint 3

If k=2k=2 does not work, either all the numbers are even or all the numbers are odd. Which kk can you try now?

Solution

Let f(k)f(k) be the number of distinct values after the operation, using kk.

Let's try k=2k=2. It works in all cases, except when either all the numbers are even or all the numbers are odd.

Let's generalize. If ai mod k=xai mod k=x, one of the following holds:

  • ai mod 2k=xai mod 2k=x;
  • ai mod 2k=x+kai mod 2k=x+k;

It means that, if f(k)=1f(k)=1 (i.e., all the values after the operations are xx), either f(2k)=1f(2k)=1 (if either all the values become xx, or they all become x+kx+k), or f(2k)=2f(2k)=2.

Therefore, it is sufficient to try k=21,…,257k=21,…,257. In fact, f(1)=1f(1)=1 and f(257)=nf(257)=n, so there must exist m<57m<57 such that f(2m)=1f(2m)=1 and f(2m+1)≠1⟹f(2m+1)=2f(2m+1)≠1⟹f(2m+1)=2.

Alternative (more intuitive?) interpretation:

  • ai mod 2jai mod 2j corresponds to the last jj digits in the binary representation of aiai. There must exist jj such that the last jj digits make exactly 22 distinct blocks.

In the following picture, a=[1005,2005,7005,11005,16005]a=[1005,2005,7005,11005,16005] and k=16k=16:

Picture

Complexity: O(nlog(maxai))O(nlog⁡(maxai))

1909C - Heavy Intervals

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Assign bigger costs to shorter intervals.

Hint 2

Solve the problem with n=2n=2.

Hint 3

Solve the following case: l=[1,2]l=[1,2], r=[3,4]r=[3,4], c=[1,2]c=[1,2]. Can you generalize it?

Solution

You have to match each lili with some rj>lirj>li.

Construct v=l1,l2,…,ln,r1,r2,…,rnv=l1,l2,…,ln,r1,r2,…,rn and sort it. If you replace every lili with the symbol (( and every riri with the symbol )), you get a regular bracket sequence (sketch of proof: li<rili<ri for each ii, so each prefix of symbols contains at least as many (( as )), so the bracket sequence is regular).

Now match each (( with the corresponding )). You can show that this is the optimal way to rearrange the lili and the riri. (From now, let the lili, riri and cici be the values after your rearrangement.)

Proof:

  • If you match the brackets in any other way, you get two intervals such that their intersection is non-empty but it is different from both intervals (i.e., you get li<lj<ri<rjli<lj<ri<rj).
  • You have also assigned some cost cici to [li,ri][li,ri] and cjcj to [lj,rj][lj,rj]. Without loss of generality, ci≤cjci≤cj (the other case is symmetrical).
  • If you swap riri and rjrj, the cost does not increase.
  • Keep swapping endpoints until you get the "regular" bracket matching. You can show that the process ends in a finite number of steps. For example, you can show that ∑((ri−li)2)∑((ri−li)2) strictly increases after each step, and it is an integer ≤∑(r2i)≤∑(ri2).
Picture

Now, you can get the minimum cost by sorting the intervals by increasing length and sorting the cici in decreasing order.

Alternative (more intuitive?) interpretation:

  • If you solve the problem with n=2n=2 and try to generalize, you can notice that it seems optimal to match every riri with the largest unused lili (if you iterate over riri in increasing order).

You can implement the solution by using either a stack (to simulate the bracket matching) or a set (to find the largest unused lili).

Complexity: O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n)

1909D - Split Plus K

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Solve the problem with k=0k=0.

Hint 2

Solve the problem with generic kk. When is the answer −1−1?

Hint 3

Do you notice any similarities between the cases with k=0k=0 and with generic kk?

Solution

Consider the "shifted" problem, where each xx on the blackboard (at any moment) is replaced with x′=x−kx′=x−k.

Now, the operation becomes "replace xx with y+zy+z such that y+z=x+k⟹(y′+k)+(z′+k)=(x′+k)+k⟹y′+z′=x′y+z=x+k⟹(y′+k)+(z′+k)=(x′+k)+k⟹y′+z′=x′". Therefore, in the shifted problem, k′=0k′=0.

Now you can replace every a′i:=ai−kai′:=ai−k with any number of values with sum a′iai′, and the answer is the amount of numbers on the blackboard at the end, minus nn.

If we want to make all the values equal to m′m′, it must be a divisor of every a′iai′.

  • If all the a′iai′ are positive, it is optimal to choose m′=gcda′im′=gcdai′.
  • If all the a′iai′ are zero, the answer is 00.
  • If all the a′iai′ are negative, it is optimal to choose m′=−gcd−a′im′=−gcd−ai′.
  • Otherwise, the answer is −1−1.

Alternative way to get this result:

  • You have to split each aiai into pipi pieces equal to mm, and their sum must be equal to ai+k(pi−1)=(ai−k)+kpi=mpiai+k(pi−1)=(ai−k)+kpi=mpi. Then, (ai−k)=(m−k)pi(ai−k)=(m−k)pi, so m′=m−km′=m−k must be a divisor of every a′i=ai−kai′=ai−k.

In both the positive and the negative case, you will only write positive elements (in the original setup), as wanted.

  • If all the a′iai′ are positive, the numbers you will write on the shifted blackboard are positive, so they will be positive also in the original blackboard.
  • If all the a′iai′ are negative, the numbers you will write on the shifted blackboard are greater than the numbers you will erase, so they will be greater than the numbers in input (and positive) in the original blackboard.

Complexity: O(n+log(maxai))O(n+log⁡(maxai))

1909E - Multiple Lamps

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Find a strategy that turns "few" lamps on in most cases.

Hint 2

Pressing all the buttons turns ⌊n−−√⌋⌊n⌋ lamps on.

Hint 3

If the strategy in Hint 2 does not work, at most 33 lamps must be on at the end.

Hint 4

Iterate over all subsets of at most 33 lamps that must be on at the end.

Solution

If you press all the buttons, lamp ii is toggled by all the divisors of ii, so it will be on if ii has an odd number of divisors, i.e., if ii is a perfect square.

Then, the strategy of pressing all the buttons works if ⌊n−−√⌋≤⌊n/5⌋⌊n⌋≤⌊n/5⌋, which is true for n≥20n≥20.

If n≤19n≤19, at most ⌊19/5⌋=3⌊19/5⌋=3 lamps must be turned on at the end.

If you know which lamps must be turned on at the end, you can iterate over the buttons from 11 to nn and press button ii if and only if lamp ii is in the wrong state. So you can iterate over all subsets of at most 33 lamps, and check if the corresponding choice of buttons is valid (i.e., the mm constraints hold). You can remove a lognlog⁡n factor by precalculating the choice of buttons for all small subsets before running the testcases.

Complexity for each test: O(∑n+(∑m⋅k2k−4))O(∑n+(∑m⋅k2k−4)) (if ⌊n/k⌋⌊n/k⌋ lamps must be on; in this case, k=5k=5).

1909F1 - Small Permutation Problem (Easy Version), 1909F2 - Small Permutation Problem (Hard Version)

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Find a clean way to visualize the problem.

Hint 2

Draw a n×nn×n grid, with tokens in (i,pi)(i,pi). Which constraints on the tokens do you have?

Hint 3

You have some "L" shapes, and each of them must contain a fixed number of tokens (in 1909F1 - Small Permutation Problem (Easy Version) the shapes are 11 cell wide and must contain at most 22 tokens). Iterate over the shapes in increasing order of ii.

Hint 4

Split each shape into 22 rectangles and iterate over the number of tokens in the first rectangle.

Solution

Draw a n×nn×n grid, with tokens in (i,pi)(i,pi).

Consider any ai≠−1ai≠−1 and the nearest aj≠−1aj≠−1 on the left (if it does not exist, let's set j=aj=0j=aj=0). Then, there must be aiai tokens in the subgrid [1,i]×[1,i][1,i]×[1,i]. We can suppose we have already inserted the ajaj tokens in [1,j]×[1,j][1,j]×[1,j], and we have to insert ai−ajai−aj tokens in the remaining cells of [1,i]×[1,i][1,i]×[1,i] (they make an "L" shape).

WLOG, the ajaj tokens in [1,j]×[1,j][1,j]×[1,j] are in the cells (1,1),…,(aj,aj)(1,1),…,(aj,aj). Then, we can put tokens in the blue cells in the picture.

Picture

The blue shape can be further split into these two rectangles:

Picture

Iterate over kk, the number of tokens in the first rectangle (0≤k≤ai−aj0≤k≤ai−aj). Then, you have to insert kk tokens into a h1×w1h1×w1 rectangle, and the remaining ai−aj−kai−aj−k tokens into a h2×(w2−k)h2×(w2−k) rectangle.

The number of ways to insert kk tokens into a h×wh×w rectangle is equal to the product of the number of ways to choose kk rows, the number of ways to choose kk columns, and the number of ways to fill the resulting k×kk×k subgrid: the result is (hk)(wk)k!(hk)(wk)k!.

an=nan=n automatically (if an≠nan≠n and an≠−1an≠−1, the answer is 00). If the non-negative aiai are non-decreasing, the sum of the ai−aj+1ai−aj+1 (i.e., the kk over which you have to iterate) is O(n)O(n), so the algorithm is efficient enough. Otherwise, the answer is 00.

Complexity: O(n)O(n)

1909G - Pumping Lemma

Author: TheScrasse
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

If you remove the condition s=x+y+zs=x+y+z, the problem becomes harder.

Hint 2

Solve for a fixed |y||y| (length of yy).

Hint 3

Suppose you have found a valid yy. Shift it one position to the right. When is it still valid?

Hint 4

The valid yy with |y|=l|y|=l start in consecutive positions.

Hint 5

Using the same idea of the proof of Hint 4, you can find all the valid yy.

Solution

Let's use s[l,r]s[l,r] to indicate the substring [l,r][l,r] of ss. Let's use (a,b)(a,b) to indicate the triple of strings (s[1,a],s[a+1,b],s[b+1,n])(s[1,a],s[a+1,b],s[b+1,n]).

Suppose (a,b)(a,b) is valid. Then, (a+1,b+1)(a+1,b+1) is valid if and only if sb+1=tb+1sb+1=tb+1.

If (a,b)(a,b) is valid, s[1,b]=t[1,b]s[1,b]=t[1,b]; so if sb≠tbsb≠tb, (a+k,b+k)(a+k,b+k) is invalid for k≥0k≥0. Therefore, if (a,b)(a,b) is the first valid pair with b−a=lb−a=l (i.e., with |y|=l|y|=l), and kk is the smallest positive integer such that (a+k,b+k)(a+k,b+k) is invalid, then sb+k≠tb+ksb+k≠tb+k, so the only valid pairs with |y|=l|y|=l are the (a+j,b+j)(a+j,b+j) with 0≤j<k0≤j<k (i.e., the valid yy with |y|=l|y|=l start in consecutive positions).

Now let's find all the valid yy with |y|=l|y|=l. Suppose (a,b)(a,b) is valid, and cc is the minimum index such that sc≠tcsc≠tc. Then, b<cb<c, and (a+k,b+k)(a+k,b+k) is valid for all b≤b+k<cb≤b+k<c. Similarly, if dd is the minimum integer such that sn−d≠tm−dsn−d≠tm−d, (a+k,b+k)(a+k,b+k) is valid for all n−d<a+k≤a⟹n−d+l<b+k≤bn−d<a+k≤a⟹n−d+l<b+k≤b.

Therefore, it's enough to check only one pair for each length, with bb in [n−d+l+1,c−1][n−d+l+1,c−1] (because either all these pairs are valid or they are all invalid). This is possible by precomputing the rolling hash of the two strings. Alternatively, you can use z-function.

Complexity: O(n)O(n)

1909H - Parallel Swaps Sort

Author: TheScrasse
Full solution: Endagorion, errorgorn
Preparation: TheScrasse, francesco

Hint 1

Find a strategy which is as simple and "easy to handle" as possible.

Hint 2

Only perform operations such that all swapped pairs have ai>ai+1ai>ai+1. Let's call such subarrays "swappable".

Hint 3

First, for each ii from left to right, do the operation on [j,i][j,i], where jj is the minimum index such that [j,i][j,i] is swappable.

Hint 4

Repeat the same algorithm from right to left.

Hint 5

After Hints 3 and 4, the array is sorted. Prove it (it will be useful).

Hint 6

Assign BB to the indices ii such that ai<ai−1ai<ai−1 and AA to the other indices. During the process in Hint 3, after the operation on index ii, which properties do AA, BB have in the prefix [1,i][1,i]?

Hint 7

Answer to Hint 6:

  • the values of type AA are increasing;
  • there are no two consecutive elements of type BB.
Hint 8

The rest of the proof (i.e., what happens during the process in Hint 4) is relatively easy.

Now let's find an efficient implementation. We have to use the properties in Hint 7.

Hint 9

You have to find the longest AB…ABAB…AB ending in position ii, and perform the operation on it. What happens during the operation?

Hint 10

AA will always remain AA. For each BB, you have to detect when it becomes AA.

Hint 11

For each BB, you can precompute the number of moves needed to make it AA.

Hint 12

For example, you can use a segment tree with the following information: the type of each element, the positions of the elements of type BB, and the number of moves required for each BB to become AA.

Solution

Let's only perform operations such that all swapped pairs have ai>ai+1ai>ai+1. Let's call such subarrays "swappable".

  • First, for each ii from left to right, do the operation on [j,i][j,i], where jj is the minimum index such that [j,i][j,i] is swappable (let's call it "operation 1.i1.i").
  • Then, for each ii from right to left, do the operation on [j,i][j,i], where jj is the minimum index such that [j,i][j,i] is swappable (let's call it "operation 2.i2.i").

After these operations, the array is sorted. Let's prove it.

Assign BB to the indices ii such that ai<ai−1ai<ai−1 and AA to the other indices. After operation 1.i1.i, only assign letters in the prefix [1,i][1,i] and ignore the other elements. During the operations 2.i2.i, assign letters to all the elements.

Most of the following proofs are by induction. After the operation 1.i1.i (supposing the properties were true after the operation 1.(i−1)1.(i−1)):

  • An element of type AA will always remain of type AA. Proof: the only elements of type AA whose previous element changes are the ones in the subarray [j,i][j,i], which are swapped with a smaller element of type BB.
  • There are no two consecutive elements of type BB. Proof: if you swap [j,i][j,i], pj−1pj−1 (if it exists) must be of type AA (otherwise [j−2,i][j−2,i] is swappable).
  • The elements of type AA are increasing. Proof: it's true if no BsBs become AsAs, and it's also true if some BsBs become AsAs because any of them is adjacent to two AsAs.

After the operation 2.i2.i:

  • The three properties above are still true.
  • The suffix [i,n][i,n] contains the values in [i,n][i,n] in order. Proof: aiai is an AA, so it must be the largest pipi in [1,i][1,i], which is ii.

Now let's understand how we can implement the algorithm. Example implementation:

  • We maintain a segment tree. The ii-th position of the segment tree contains information about the element which was initially pipi. Note that the relative position of BsBs never changes: for example, if you want information about the last kk BsBs in the current permutation, and you search them in the segment tree, you will find exactly the last kk BsBs, even though their indices will not correspond to the current indices.
  • We have to find the longest swappable subarray ending at ii. It means we need the current positions of the BsBs. For each BB maintain the current position, and assume the position of all the AsAs is 00. Also maintain, for each element, if it is a BB or not. Note that the BsBs that are affected by each operation can be found in a suffix of the segment tree.
  • In this way, finding the longest swappable subarray can be done with a binary search on the segment tree: since BsBs cannot be consecutive, you have to find the longest suffix such that the sum of the positions of the BsBs is the maximum possible (i.e., if there are kk BsBs and the last of them is in position ii, the sum of their positions must be k(i−k+1)k(i−k+1)).
  • After finding the longest subarray in the segment tree, you have to perform the operation on it, i.e., subtract 11 from all the nonzero positions.
  • Some BsBs may become AsAs. How to detect them? Since AsAs never become BsBs, a BB becomes AA after it is swapped with all the elements greater than it on its left. So you can precompute the number of swaps that every BB needs to become AA, and put it in the segment tree as well. Again, the operation is "subtract 11 from a range".
  • Detecting AsAs means detecting elements which need 00 swaps to become AsAs. You can find them after each operation by traversing the segment tree (which must support "range min" on the number of swaps needed), and set their position to 00 and the number of swaps needed to ∞∞.

Complexity: 2n−32n−3 moves, O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) time.

1909I - Short Permutation Problem

Author: TheScrasse
Full solution: errorgorn
Preparation: TheScrasse

Hint 1

Insert the elements into the permutation in some comfortable order.

Hint 2

Suppose mm is even. You can insert elements in the order [m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…][m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…].

Hint 3

You can solve for a single mm with DP. Can you calculate the DP for multiple mm simultaneously?

Hint 4

The first part of the DP (where you insert both "small" and "big" elements) is the same for each mm (but with a different length), and for the second part (where you only insert "small" elements) you don't need DP.

Hint 5

The second part of the DP can be replaced with combinatorics formulas.

Hint 6

Binomials are compatible with NTT.

Solution

Let's solve for a single mm. Suppose mm is even. Start from an empty array, and insert the elements in the order [m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…][m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…]. At any moment, all the elements are concatenated, and you can insert new elements either at the beginning, at the end or between two existing elements.

  • When you insert an element ≥m/2≥m/2, the sum with any of the previous inserted elements is ≥m≥m.
  • Otherwise, the sum is <m<m.

So you can calculate dpi,j=dpi,j= number of ways to insert the first ii elements (of [m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…][m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…]) and make jj "good" pairs (with sum ≥m≥m).

You can split the ordering [m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…][m/2,m/2−1,m/2+1,m/2−2,…] into two parts:

  • small and big elements alternate;
  • there are only small elements.

For the second part, you don't need DP. Suppose you have already inserted ii elements, and there are jj good pairs, but when you will have inserted all elements you want kk good pairs. The number of ways to insert the remaining elements can be computed with combinatorics in O(1)O(1) after precomputing factorials and inverses (you have to choose which pairs to break and use stars and bars; we skip exact formulas because they are relatively easy to find).

If you rearrange the factorials correctly, you can get that all the answers for a fixed mm can be computed by multiplying two polynomials, one of which contains the dpi,jdpi,j, where ii is equal to the length of the "alternating" prefix. NTT is fast enough.

Complexity: O(n2logn)O(n2log⁡n)

Tutorial of Pinely Round 3 (Div. 1 + Div. 2)
tutorial
  • +354
  • TheScrasse
  • 2 years ago
  • 212

gas
2 years ago, show (+6) #
»
gas
2 years ago, hide # |
 
0

Can someone explain why this solution receives TLE? I assumed that the time complexity would simply be O(nlogn): 238557809

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    kamilszymczak1
    2 years ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    kamilszymczak1
    2 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +53

    That's because you've used

    upper_bound(r.begin(), r.end(), l[i]);

    instead of

    r.upper_bound(r[i]);

    The first one works in O(n)O(n) and the second one in O(logn)O(logn) where nn is the size of the set. This is due to the fact that the first function is a generic function that works with any container and is guaranteed to work in O(logn)O(logn) only if the container supports random access (std::set doesn't support random access), while the second one is a specific function designed only for sets and is guaranteed to have good complexity. You can refer to the documentation in case you're confused:

    • This one if for the generic upper_bound

    • This one if for the set-specific upper_bound

    → Reply

maroonrk
2 years ago, show #
»
maroonrk
2 years ago, hide # |
 
+130

I have a different solution for H so I'll leave it here.

Let's assume NN is even. Consider NN operations (1,N),(2,N−1),(1,N),⋯,(2,N−1)(1,N),(2,N−1),(1,N),⋯,(2,N−1). These operations reverse the whole permutation. For odd NN, one can do similar operations.

During this reversing process, every pair of numbers become adjacent at some moment. Therefore we can "insert" adjacent-swap operations to achieve the arbitrary-swap operation in the final array.

The pitfall is that when we try to do multiple arbitrary swaps, they can interfere with each other during the reversing process. However, if the target permutation has a matching structure, there are no worries about interference.

Then, remember that we can obtain any permutation by composing two appropriate matching permutations. Therefore by repeating the reverse-and-swap process twice one can sort the input permutation.

The time complexity is O(N)O(N) and it's better than the intended solution. However, the number of moves is 3N+const3N+const and it's worse than 2N2N. I'm so glad that the author didn't ask me to optimize this constant. (By the way, I'm wondering if it's possible to achieve a better constant factor with my approach.)

→ Reply
»

      first, every number % gcd would be same.

      second, if every number % (gcd * 2) would be same, then gcd will not be gcd, it should be at least 2 * gcd.

          »
          lisashi
          2 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          +2

          for example,

          7 10 16

          the gcd of the difference is 3

          So we know that after mod 3, they will be same. i.e. 1 1 1

          If we look at gcd * 2, i.e. 6. we can see the result would be 1 4 4. There could be only two elements in it. Also, if everything are still the same, then we must be have a wrong gcd in the first step.

        619

        619
        2 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        Hello, Sorry if my doubt sounds dumb but I agree that everynumber%(gcd*2) won't be the same but how can you claim that those "not same" %(gcd*2)s will be "same" as there have to be exactly 2 numbers one would be those who are still %(gcd*2)==0 and the other ones who are not zero but how can you claim that the other ones who are not zero will be equal

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          lisashi
          2 years ago, show (+4) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          lisashi
          2 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          +1

          let's say every number mod gcd is x, i.e. a[i] = x + num * gcd for some integer num

          then everything mod 2 * gcd would be either x, or x + gcd.

          This is because when num is even, a[i] % (2 * gcd) would be still x,

          when num is odd, a[i] % (2 * gcd) would be x + gcd

          → Reply

0

How to solve F1 with combinatorics?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Jayg000e
    2 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Jayg000e
    2 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +17

    A way to think of this is to maintain the invariant that the difference of p between consecutive elements in front of the ith element is correct and also to satisfy the current consecutive difference. Denote unallocated elements smaller than i as k.

    If we want to make a consecutive difference of 2, we must assign the current largest element in previous k-1 vacant place, and place any of other k-1 element in current place which is (k-1)*(k-1) ways. Note that placing the current largest element in front of ith element will not break the difference invariant.

    If we want to make a consecutive difference of 1, we can either put the current largest in the previous k-1 vacancy or place any of current k element in current place. This results in 2k-1 ways. As mentioned before, placing the current largest element in front of ith element will not break the invariant.

    → Reply
»
drexdelta
2 years ago, show (+1) #
»
drexdelta
2 years ago, hide # |

can someone please explain first three lines of solution of problem D ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    moradiya84
    2 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    moradiya84
    2 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    let's forget array and assume that we have to deal with single element.

    if this element is greater than k say (k+x),now in one operation we have to find 2 number a,b such that a+b=(k)+(k+x), to reduce complexity of question we are doing this trick

    a+b=(k)+(k+x)=> (a-k)+(b-k)=(0)+(x)

    we don't care about final number we care about how many operation so we reduce all number by k so our operation changed in select number and break it such in two number such that sum is our x((k+x)-(k)).

    in short after reducing by k we don't have to deal with k;)

    → Reply
»

    lov.ish

  • »
    »
    beaten_by_ai
    2 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    beaten_by_ai
    2 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +1

    Assume we create a new board BB, on which for each number xx written on our original board (call it AA), we write the number x−kx−k on BB. Then, let's try seeing what our operation does. So, since y+z=x+k⟹(y−k)+(z−k)=(x−k)y+z=x+k⟹(y−k)+(z−k)=(x−k) (for the original board), and since we've written y−k,z−ky−k,z−k on our modified board instead of x−kx−k, this gives the observation that on BB, our operation just corresponds to replacing x∗x∗ by two numbers y∗,z∗y∗,z∗ such that x∗=y∗+z∗x∗=y∗+z∗. Then, solving this reduced problem is pretty easy (for implementation purposes, just subtract kk from each element of aa, and do the calculations on that itself).

    → Reply

        well it is kind of obvious intuition you get once u see 2 distinct values modulo some k you think of 2 since only two values are possible however then you remmeber all values might be pair hence there is only one value and then this amazing idea gets to u about the next power of 2 which is 4 well since the numbers were allwith the same parity they have only 2 values possible (pair numbers possible values are 2 or 4 and impairs values are 1 and 3) and then again there is possiblity that they all have same value time to check 8 again only 2 values are possible, you can conlcude that the sol requires checking all powers of 2,genius,see this much easier than what the editorial makes it look like.By the way i just guided you trough how the logical thinking went in my brain

        → Reply

      +13

      (Nvm, this is wrong if you run it on a decreasing array.)

      By the way, isn't the number of moves for the editorial solution actually bounded above by 3n/23n/2? Since the number of moves during the second phase is at most the number of Bs after the first phase, which is at most n/2n/2 due to there being no two consecutive Bs and the last letter being an A.

      Benq
      2 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 3 →  
      0

      Also curious about these:

      • Were you aware of a solution to H that made 3n3n operations before the contest? Or did the limits just happen to be large enough to allow that to pass?

      • What was the original solution to H with O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) operations?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        ffao
        2 years ago, show (+4) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        ffao
        2 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +10

        H with O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) operations sounds like a considerably more boring problem, as I believe you can simply simulate merge sort: suppose the right part starts at xx, then you can use operations on [x−1,x][x−1,x], [x−2,x+1][x−2,x+1] etc to create a train of moving elements from right to left, and you remove elements from the train when they reach their final positions.

        Other than the very annoying implementation, H with O(n)O(n) operations is a work of art :)

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Benq
          2 years ago, show (+3) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Benq
          2 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          Hm, I don't fully understand.

          By merge sort, do you mean that given two sorted arrays on consecutive ranges, you can merge them into one sorted array?

          If you do operations on [x−1,x],[x−2,x+1],[x−3,x+2],…[x−1,x],[x−2,x+1],[x−3,x+2],… then this will move some elements right to left over xx and others left to right over xx. But how are you removing elements when they reach their final positions? What if there's some element in the middle of the train that you don't want to keep moving?

          → Reply
          • »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            Endagorion
            2 years ago, show (+2) #
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            Endagorion
            2 years ago, hide # ^ |
             
            0

            Here's how I did it: imagine inserting just the rightmost element aiai of the left half with [x−1,x][x−1,x] operations. ai−1ai−1 can trail behind two positions away for free simply by extending the segments to the left (skipping the first one). We stop the trail early if ai−1ai−1 doesn't need to go that far, and proceed to ai−2ai−2. Final case is if ai−1ai−1 needs land next to aiai, in which case we add one more swap at the end to achieve that.

            → Reply
            • »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              Benq
              2 years ago, show (+1) #
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              Benq
              2 years ago, hide # ^ |
              ← Rev. 4 →  
              0

              I agree about aiai and ai−1ai−1, but what if ai−2ai−2 and aiai need to keep moving to the right but you don't want ai−1ai−1 to keep moving to the right?

              It's true that aiai must move at least as far right as ai−1ai−1, but once you take into account the fact that ai−1ai−1 must trail two spaces behind aiai to move them rightward at the same speed, this is no longer true.

              → Reply
              • »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                Endagorion
                2 years ago, show #
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                »
                Endagorion
                2 years ago, hide # ^ |
                 
                +10

                For ai−2ai−2 we can forget what aiai does, and focus on operations moving ai−1ai−1. Since they don't want to swap, the same logic keeps applying.

                → Reply

        TheScrasse

        TheScrasse
        2 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        +10
        • I was not aware of any linear solution other than the official one. I didn't put 2n2n as the operation limit to avoid spoilers.
        • The original solution was "put the n/2n/2 smallest elements in the left half of the array in O(n)O(n) operations, then recurse". Some testers found the "merge sort" solution explained by ffao.
        → Reply

+8

Alternative solution for E:

Let's call a subset SS of pressed buttons good if at the end at most ⌊n5⌋⌊n5⌋ lamps turned on. For n≤19n≤19, the amount of such subsets is ≤1159≤1159. We can find them for each n∈[1,19]n∈[1,19] in O(∑n=1192n⋅nlogn)O(∑n=1192n⋅nlog⁡n), and then check only them for each n≤19n≤19 testcase in O(nm+1159n)O(nm+1159n).

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Bungmint
    2 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Bungmint
    2 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +33

    Actually, you can prove that the number of such subset for n=19n=19 is exactly (193)+(192)+(191)=1159(193)+(192)+(191)=1159 since we can think of each switch ii associated with a vector vi∈Z192vi∈Z219 and all the vectors are independent, so v1v1, ……, v19v19 is the basis, and we only care when ⊕kj=1vij⊕j=1kvij has less than or equal to 33 bits on (excluding the case it is equal to zero), so we get the above result.

    → Reply

In problem C, making small intervals is not the same as sorting the sequences of left and right endpoints and pairing the corresponding indices. Let, u=L1,L2,...,Lnu=L1,L2,...,Ln si obtained after sorting l1,l2,...,lnl1,l2,...,ln and v=R1,R2,...,Rnv=R1,R2,...,Rn is obtained after sorting r1,r2,...,rnr1,r2,...,rn. Then the new intervals will be [L1,R1],[L2,R2],...,[Ln,Rn][L1,R1],[L2,R2],...,[Ln,Rn].

        TheScrasse
        2 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        Your approach fails for l=[1,2]l=[1,2], r=[3,4]r=[3,4], c=[1,2]c=[1,2]. You claim that it's optimal to keep everything as it is, while the editorial makes l=[1,2]l=[1,2], r=[4,3]r=[4,3], c=[1,2]c=[1,2] (33 is matched with the closest lili, which is 22). The picture "explains" why the second solution is better than the first.

          Hi, I didn't understand the picture can you explain it once? what is the 3 and 5 and what the different colors represent

          → Reply
          • »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            TheScrasse
            2 years ago, show #
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            TheScrasse
            2 years ago, hide # ^ |
            ← Rev. 2 →  
            0

            The red subinterval is the only one whose cost changes (from 55 to 33). In general, you want intervals with big cost to be as small as possible, so you let intervals with small cost "steal" subintervals from intervals with larger cost.

            → Reply

I was thinking of the following test cases

It should seem to always be possible for k to be a single digit value unless all numbers are multiples of 10.

If there are 2 distinct numbers for each last digit, k=10 is always a solution.

If there are more than 2 distinct numbers for each last digit, we can iterate k=2-9 to find a value for which it works

Golovanov399
2 years ago, show #
»
Golovanov399
2 years ago, hide # |
 
+10

I have another solution for problem G. It is easier than the intended one in my opinion, at least idea-wise.

Solution

Let d=m−nd=m−n. Fix any valid xx, yy and zz. Let's see what is going on closely. If i=|x|i=|x| then the substring t[i,i+d)t[i,i+d) is yk−1yk−1. In particular, it has period |y||y| and for at least |y||y| indices jj, starting from ii, the equality tj=tj+dtj=tj+d holds (because another occurrence of yy follows t[i,i+d)t[i,i+d)). Obviously, the opposite works: if, for some ii and ll, the substring t[i,i+d)t[i,i+d) is t[i,i+l)t[i,i+l) repeated several times, and for all jj such that i≤j<i+li≤j<i+l we have tj=tj+dtj=tj+d, then this pair (i,l)(i,l) corresponds to a unique valid triple x=t[0,i)=s[0,i)x=t[0,i)=s[0,i), y=t[i,i+l)=s[i,i+l)y=t[i,i+l)=s[i,i+l), z=s[i+l,n)z=s[i+l,n). We are going to find the number of valid ll for each 0≤i≤n0≤i≤n. The plan is the following.

First, we find for how many first indices jj from [i,i+d)[i,i+d) the equality tj=tj+dtj=tj+d holds. This can be done for all ii in one iteration over string dd from right to left. Denote this number by cc (stands for common).

After we established this, we will find the smallest pp (stands for period) such that t[i,i+d)t[i,i+d) is t[i,i+p)t[i,i+p) repeated d/pd/p times. Then the number of good ll for this ii will equal the number of gg such that p∣g∣dp∣g∣d and g≤cg≤c, or, equivalently, the number of g≤c/pg≤c/p for which g∣d/pg∣d/p.

To find the period pp, we will try to find it under the assumption that p≤d/2p≤d/2, and if we fail, then p=dp=d.

Let's solve a subtask: given string uu of length dd, find its period if it's at most d/2d/2. Let ii and jj be any pair of integers that 0≤i0≤i, j≤dj≤d and j−i=⌊d/2⌋j−i=⌊d/2⌋. Then the period is min(d,lcm(tailperiod(u[0,j)),tailperiod(u[i,d))))min(d,lcm(tailperiod(u[0,j)),tailperiod(u[i,d)))), where tailperiod(v)tailperiod(v) is the smallest pp such that vv is a prefix of the infinite concatenation of v[0,p)v[0,p) with itself (it is like a period, except there may be some leftover, although also consistent with the periodicity). The proof is left as an exercise for the reader.

To always have such pair of indices, let's find such periods for all t[i,j)t[i,j) where ii is divisible by ⌊d/2⌋⌊d/2⌋ and j−i≤dj−i≤d. We can find them by running z-function for each substring starting at such ii of length dd (or till the end of tt, whichever is shorter). Similarly, we can run z-function on similar substrings of tt reversed, which concludes this part of the solution.

What remains is to be able to tell how many divisors of g∣pg∣p don't exceed some cc, where p∣dp∣d. I just generated all divisors for each p∣dp∣d beforehand, and solved this subtask using lower_bound. I suspect that it can be shown that pp in these queries first only decreases, then only increases, in which case we can re-generate the divisors every time it changes, which will be better than loglog per query.

→ Reply
»

I think it should be y+z = x+k instead of x+y = z+k.

      0mar

      0mar
      2 years ago, hide # ^ |

      Can you explain how the rearrangement inequality is related?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        0mar
        2 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        0mar
        2 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        I guess I actually do see how it is used in the problem (in sorting the cost array in descending order with the lengths), but that wasn't the hard part for me. The part that was hard was how to pick the elements l and r. I guess an intuitive explanation would be that you want to maximize the lengths so that you can pair a higher length with a a lower cost. To do that, for the smallest Rs, you want to pick and L that is the closest to them because you want to save the smaller Ls for the Rs that are larger, because that results in a larger lengths. I can elaborate more on this if anyone wants me to.

        → Reply

[2,4] ci=2 [1,3] ci=1 now cost is 6 then ** If you swap ri and rj, the cost does not increase. ** swapping here will make the cost 7 so I don't get it can you please explain what is wrong here

    For each test case, you have to iterate over O(nk−2)=O(k2k−4)O(nk−2)=O(k2k−4) masks (in the worst case), and for each of them you can check if it works in O(m)O(m).

        TheScrasse
        2 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        +8

        tl;dr replace 55 with kk and 33 with k−2k−2 in the editorial.

        The largest solution to ⌊n/k⌋<⌊n−−√⌋⌊n/k⌋<⌊n⌋ is n=k(k−1)−1n=k(k−1)−1. In that case, you already have a solution which turns ⌊k(k−1)−1−−−−−−−−−−√⌋≤k−1⌊k(k−1)−1⌋≤k−1 lamps on, so you can iterate over masks which contain k−2k−2 lamps.

For D, the shifting part is still unclear to me(ik what is being done ,but how is it correct and how to think of it) thnx in advance if someone can spare their time or share related resources

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    nzo
    2 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    nzo
    2 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I was really struggling for a while to come to grips with this solution and I think I am now (mostly) convinced of why it works, so I'll try to explain my way of thinking about it.

    Imagine a number line with numbers x, y and z marked such that y + z = x + k.

    Now think about the operation you can do: y + z = x + k. Going back to the number line, what this operation means is you would have to pick x and move it k unities to the right, then pick two numbers x and y such that their sum "lands" in (x + k) and not x.

    For each of those three numbers now, mark in the number line its value shifted k unities to the left, and call these new guys x', y' and z'. Clearly you can represent x by (x' + k), y by (y' + k) and z by (z' + k). So, looking again at our operation, we have (y' + k) + (z' + k) = (x' + k) + k -> y' + z' = x'.

    What that tells us is that if we had a magical way of taking every number on our line and shifting it k unities to the left, when we would execute the operation on an original number x, instead of doing that complicated process (moving it k unities to the right and only then finding numbers which add to it), we could map x to its representation in the shifted "world" and find two other numbers, also in the shifted world, such that their sum is equal to the representation of x.

    Our magical way of doing this shift is simply to take every number given in the input and subtract it by k. Now, for every operation we do in those numbers (including gcd), we are working with numbers from our shifted world; so we can now solve the problem as if k didn't even exist. Hope this is helpful in some way :)

    → Reply
»

In D you can also do the reverse problem: Start at the final state which is a bunch of identical elements then apply "combine and subtract kk" operations and arrive at a similar solution with algebra.

0

problem D is amazing i have different approach for. suppose mn and mx are the max and min elements if mx == mn ans is 0. if mn <= k && k <= mx we can neve the values of array to a single value. if k < mn || k > mx ans will exist. assume at the end all the elements are equal to x so the last element we remove = 2 * x — k. second last element we would remove will be either 2 * x — k or 3 * x — k and so on. it shows that all the elements in the initial array is of the form .. pi * x — (pi — 1) * k, after rearranging pi(x — k) + k. now ans would be just summation of all pi.

→ Reply

For a newbie like me, who are struggling to understand problem B

The Last Digits Picture Write every number in binary. Now look at only the last j digits of each number. j=1: last 1 bit → possible values: 0, 1 j=2: last 2 bits → possible values: 00, 01, 10, 11 j=3: last 3 bits → possible values: 000, 001, 010, ... Key observation: if all numbers share the same last j bits, say they all end in 1 — then at j+1, each number can only end in 01 or 11. Still at most 2 distinct endings! So once you're at 1 distinct group, the next step gives you 1 or 2 — never 3+. Keep increasing j until you first see 2 distinct endings. Done. Example: a = [1005, 2005, 7005, 11005, 16005]

j=1 → 1, 1, 1, 1, 1 → 1 distinct ✗ j=2 → 01, 01, 01, 01, 01 → 1 distinct ✗ j=3 → 101, 101, 101, 101, 101 → 1 distinct ✗ j=4 → 1101, 1101, 1101, 1101, 0101 → 2 distinct ✓ answer = 2⁴ = 16 Complexity: O(n log maxA)

→ Reply
